coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot/polymer nanocomposites: From green synthesis to energy, environmental and biomedical applications

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), a novel family of multifunctional carbon-nanomaterials, has the potential to revolutionize several important fields supporting the transformation to sustainable bioeconomy. CDs are characterized by attractive properties such as tunable optoelectronic and fluorescence properties, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, bioactivity and superior water solubility. Diverse methods and precursors have been applied for the synthesis of CDs. The possibility to scale-up the synthesis of CDs by employing Green Chemistry principles is a current research hotspot and a prerequisite for large-scale production and applications. Incorporation of CDs into polymer matrices, is another emerging research area with wide potential application range. This approach endows additional desirable functions and enables easy handling and reuse. This review summarizes recent progress regarding the green synthesis of CDs, the fundamental strategies for the construction of CD/polymer nanocomposites and the state-of-art applications of the CD/polymer nanocomposites in the realm of energy storage, environment and biomedicine. Finally, the outlook including challenges and future potential of CDs and CD/polymer composites are discussed.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot/polymer nanocomposites: From green synthesis to energy, environmental and biomedical applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), a novel family of multifunctional carbon-nanomaterials, has the potential to revolutionize several important fields supporting the transformation to sustainable bioeconomy. CDs are characterized by attractive properties such as tunable optoelectronic and fluorescence properties, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, bioactivity and superior water solubility. Diverse methods and precursors have been applied for the synthesis of CDs. The possibility to scale-up the synthesis of CDs by employing Green Chemistry principles is a current research hotspot and a prerequisite for large-scale production and applications. Incorporation of CDs into polymer matrices, is another emerging research area with wide potential application range. This approach endows additional desirable functions and enables easy handling and reuse. This review summarizes recent progress regarding the green synthesis of CDs, the fundamental strategies for the construction of CD/polymer nanocomposites and the state-of-art applications of the CD/polymer nanocomposites in the realm of energy storage, environment and biomedicine. Finally, the outlook including challenges and future potential of CDs and CD/polymer composites are discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: The global challenges with respect to energy, environment and health have driven the evolution of new materials and techniques to conserve energy, boost a sustainable ecosystem and promote human well-being [1]. Carbon nanomaterials are a burgeoning family of new materials with large potential to positively influence both science and society. However, the synthesis of nanomaterials often requires high energy consumption, involvement of toxic chemical reagents and fossil fuel-based raw materials, countering the principles of green chemistry [2]. Green synthesis processes and utilization of renewable resources are required to release the full potential of these materials.

Carbon dots (CDs) as one of the most promising class of carbon nanomaterials have attracted large attention since discovery in 2004 during the separation and purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes [3]. CDs are typically quasi-spherical nanoparticles with the average size less than 10 nm [4]. Based on the specific carbon core structure and surface groups, CDs can be classified into carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene or graphene oxide quantum dots (GQDs or GOQDs) and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [5]. Due to their quantum size effect, surface defect state, and the band gap transitions of conjugated π-domains, CDs demonstrate strong and tunable fluorescence property, unique up-conversion photoluminescence, excellent photostability and electro-excited charge transfer performance [6]. This enables their applications in the fields of sensors, optronics, energy storage and photocatalysis. CDs with small size can due to their fluorescence, good biocompatibility, low toxicity and hydrophilic nature be applied in the biomedical applications such as bio-sensing [7], bio-imaging [8], bio-labeling [9], and drug delivery [10]. It has also been shown that CDs could work as bioactivity booster to induce cell growth or mineralization on the scaffold materials [11,12]. In addition, the large specific surface area and abundant surficial functionalities as well as rich π-domains allow CDs to effectively interact with organic molecules and metals ions, leading to potential applications in wastewater remediation [13–16]. At present, a broad variety of synthesis methods have been developed to produce CDs, which can be typically classified as top-down and bottom-up strategies [17–23]. Unfortunately, many current methods require toxic chemical reagents and solvents, high temperatures, long reaction times, and complicated processing steps. Besides, non-renewable or expensive carbon sources are often needed particularly during production by top-down methods. Development of green chemistry concepts to fabricate fluorescent CDs through facile, economical, and sustainable routes is therefore of high interest.

While seeking green ways to synthesize CDs, it's also worth exploring the ways to stabilize CDs to fully release the potential of CDs in the above-mentioned applications. Fabrication of the CD-based nanocomposite materials by integrating CDs into compatible solid matrices through intermolecular interactions or covalent bonding is a feasible pathway. Correspondingly, for many applications polymers are ideal solid matrices. Correctly selected polymer matrix can play crucial role in dispersedly anchoring CDs to effectively avoid the aggregation-induced photoluminescence (PL) self-quenching of CDs [24]. Furthermore, this approach can control the nanocomposite macro-morphology and offer additional advantages such as improved portability, self-supporting ability, rapid electron or thermal conductivity and enhanced chemical stability for the CDs. Meanwhile, the unique features of CDs can grant polymers with other potential capabilities including promoted mechanical strength, excellent fluorescence performance, ionic conductivity, bioactivity, self-healing, and stimuli-responsive characteristic [25–30]. These multifunctional CD/polymer nanocomposite materials possess broad application prospects in diverse fields, ranging from portable contaminant detectors [31], adsorbents [14], batteries [27], supercapacitors [32], solar cells [33], biomedical scaffolds [34], and drug carriers [35]. In comparison with other types of carbon/polymer composite materials such as graphene, graphene oxide and carbon nanotube polymer nanocomposites, the unique advantages of CD/polymer nanocomposite lie in (1) the much broader choices of CD precursors- in addition to non-renewable resources, a wide range of sustainable biomass resources including e.g. industrial and agricultural side-products can be utilized. Also the CD synthesis methods are normally milder, (2) the distinct optical properties of CDs, endowing composite materials with specific applications in environmental and biomedical fields, (3) the generally smaller size of CDs leading to the larger specific surface area to interact with the polymer matrix and (4) CDs typically have better biocompatibility compared to e.g. carbon nanotubes.

With the focus on CD/polymer nanocomposites, this review aims to demonstrate a systematic picture from the synthesis of the basic building blocks -CDs to the construction of nanocomposite materials. Compared with some previous reviews which focused on summarizing the conventional synthesis approach of CDs and their fundamental properties, the possible mechanisms of PL and the fluorescence-relevant applications [36–38], we highlight the production of CDs from low-cost biomass resources or by green synthesis techniques. Further, the principles for construction of CDs/polymer nanocomposites are overviewed and it is also worth noting that the construction of nanocomposite materials containing biobased CDs and eco-friendly biopolymers/biodegradable polymers are specially listed. Moreover, the recent progress in some cutting-edge applications of the CD/polymer nanocomposites in the fields of environment, energy storage and biomedicine are discussed. Finally, the outlook on the future directions and the current challenges in this topic are provided to open up new angles for exploiting greener ways for CD/polymer nanocomposites fabrications as well as expanding their application scope.

In general, chemical and physical characteristics, such as the type and abundance of chemical functionalities and morphology, and the subsequent material properties are the crucial factors for successful applications of materials. The role of synthesis process is thus crucial. The developed methods for the synthesis of CDs can be divided into two groups, namely “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches as demonstrated in Fig. 1 . The top-down approach refers to breaking down the bulky carbon structures such as graphite, carbon nanotube, graphene oxide into the nano-sized material with the dimension less than 10 nm via employing for example laser ablation [4], arc discharge [3], and electrochemical techniques [18]. The bottom-up approach instead involves synthesizing CDs from small organic precursor molecules or polymers with the merits of being suitable for large-scale production, with wide choice of CD precursors and designable structures and properties. This can involve dehydration, polymerization and carbonization processes via for example thermal decomposition [39], microwave pyrolysis [40], plasma treatment [41], and hydrothermal/acidic oxidation [42]. More recently efforts were made to increase the sustainability by production more in-line with green chemistry principles. So far, this largely centered on utilization of green carbon sources i.e., biomass-based resources and development of greener synthesis processes.

Under the framework of selecting suitable carbon precursors to synthesize CDs, biomass has unique advantages such as nontoxicity, renewability, wide availability, and low cost. The utilization of biomass supports bioeconomy— an economy model where the basic building blocks for energy, materials, and chemicals are derived from renewable biological resources [43]. The replacement of conventional precursors with biomass-based carbon sources is an important step forward, but still high temperatures or long reaction times can reduce the sustainability. However, CDs with variable structures and properties can be obtained due to the diversity of natural biomass. HTC and the microwave-assisted HTC techniques work well for transforming biomass feedstock in wet state into CDs, which saves the biomass pre-drying process [44–46]. Pyrolysis carbonization is another widely-used technique for the CD synthesis, but it requires the biomass feedstock to be dry [47]. Microwave irradiation technique has been shown to be effective in converting carbohydrates, amino acid and ascorbic acid into the CDs and it also works well for biomass-residues or industrial side-products such as palm kernel shell [48], mint leaves [49], kelp [50] and empty fruit bunch biochar [51]. There are also other CD synthesis methods such as chemical oxidation, and ultrasonic treatment, however with no specific requirement in the CD precursor's type [52–53]. Currently, the promising biomass-based carbon sources can be categorized as plant waste and animal waste or their extract-based resources.

The attention has turned to synthesize CDs from plant residues due to the economic and environmental exigency. Utilization of cheap waste products for producing high-value-added low-dimensional carbon materials with an underlying potential for the industrial scale-up has high potential, especially if facile and cost-effective production processes can be developed. Compared with other techniques which normally require a preheat-drying treatment of the raw biomass residues, the hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) technique developed by Bergius in 1913 is capable of converting biomass directly to carbon-rich products [54]. In this process water acts both as the reactant medium and reagent. Lotus, camellia and rapeseed flower pollens were utilized as the carbon precursors to synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs via scaled-up HTC at 180 °C for 24 h [55]. These CDs of 1–2 nm in size manifested excellent PL intensity and biocompatibility, indicating promising applications in e.g. bio-imaging. Sun et al. [56] synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQD) from rice husks with a yield of 15 wt%. A narrow size distribution of CDs between 3 and 6 nm was measured with TEM. The abundant waste from Sago industry in the Southeast Asia could be transformed into fluorescent CDs, which showed potential applications in the heavy metal ion detection [57]. Grass [58] and bamboo leaves [59] were utilized to produce well-defined carbonaceous nanoparticles by hydrolysis, dehydration, fragmentation, polymerization, condensation and aromatization. Moreover, various type of fruits and extracts from banana [60], apple [61], papaya [62], peach [63], lemon [64], cherry tomatoes [65] and mangosteen pulp [66] were also proven as suitable precursors for functional CDs. We previously transformed series of plant-based biopolymers or waste products such as cellulose [67], starch [68], lignin [69], waste paper [67], and spend coffee grounds [70] into the nano-graphene oxide-type CDs (nGO-CDs) via two-step process consisting of microwave-assisted HTC and exfoliation-oxidation treatment. Subsequently the reduced nano-graphene oxide-type CDs (r-nGO-CDs) could be generated via microwave-assisted HTC process in superheated water with or without caffeic acid as reducing agent [71]. nGO-CDs formed a yellow suspension in water, whereas after the microwave treatment, the r-nGO-CDs suspension showed a black color, visually indicating the reduction of oxygen-containing groups of GOQD. The appearance of π-π* transition in the XPS spectrum of r-nGO-CDs could imply the recovery of sp2 conjugated structures. Normally the size of nGO-CDs and r-nGO-CDs ranging from several nanometers to a few tens of nanometer, mainly depended on the biomass source and the parameters such as reaction time and temperature during the microwave-assisted HTC process. nGO-CDs demonstrated pH-dependent self-assembly behavior in aqueous solution and concentration-dependent self-association behavior when incorporated into the polymer matrix to form e.g., spheres and stacked 2D sheets, paving the way to tune the morphology of the nanocomposite [11,72] .

Animal waste is also a vast treasure trove for the synthesis of CDs. Hair fibers with the main component-keratin has been considered as low-cost renewable precursors for carbon materials. Keratin consists of polypeptide chain with disulfide linkages, which benefits the synthesis of sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs (S,N-CDs). The higher temperature favored the formation of S,N-CDs with smaller size, higher S content and longer emission wavelengths from PL. Besides, the S,N-CDs also demonstrated high solubility, low toxicity and good biocompatibility. S,N-CDs were successfully produced from hair fibers of humans [73–74], sheep and pigs [75]. These S,N-CDs showed high pH stability, ionic tolerance and relatively high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 29.0%, 25.6% and 20.1%, respectively, depending on the precursor. A microwave-assisted hydrothermal approach was applied on goose feather to generate CDs with a diameter of ~21.5 nm and height of ~4.5 nm. The resulting CDs contained oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur atoms, and demonstrated a QY of 17.1% [76]. The residues from seafood industries including fish scale, crab shell and prawn shell can also be utilized to synthesize CDs [10,77,78]. N-doped fluorescent CDs prepared from fish scale showed a homogeneous size distribution with a diameter of ~2 nm and relatively high nitrogen content of 14.6%, which is higher than most values reported for N-doped CDs [79]. The crab, prawn or shrimp shells are the natural source of chitin or chitosan, which is the second most abundant polysaccharide next to cellulose, consisting of randomly arranged β (1–4) linked D-glucosamine and N-acetyl-D glucosamine units. Microwave-assisted hydrothermal or pyrolysis treatments have been applied on raw shrimp shells and chitosan for the fabrication of CDs, which were magneto-fluorescent [80]. Although shrimp shells and chitosan contain abundant N elements, Feng and co-workers found that after the microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment of chitosan, the generated CDs contain less amount of N, which was around 6.1% [81]. This was rationally explained by the replacement of -NH2 with OH ions to form glucose in an acidic aqueous environment. By contrast, the high-temperature pyrolysis on shrimp shells led to the formation of N-doped CDs, with the N content reaching 11.8% [82]. In addition, pigskin and wool were also utilized to produce CDs, with the QY of 24.1%, and 16.3% respectively [83,84].

Green synthesis process should avoid or limit the need of toxic chemicals, long reaction time, high pressure and temperature, and the tedious preparation step. This also provides economic and environmental benefits as compared to the conventional chemical and physical methods. Currently, the green synthesis process for production of CD mainly include hydrothermal carbonization, microwave irradiation, ultrasound treatment, ozone/hydrogen peroxide oxidation, and self-exothermic synthesis.

Hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) approach is one of the most widely used approaches for the CD synthesis. It is a thermochemical conversion process to transform the wet biomass feedstock into carbonized structures with no dependency on energy input, which otherwise is required for pre-drying the biomass before processing. Commonly, during the HTC process, the temperature ranges are controlled between 180 and 250 °C in water (subcritical temperatures), which in this content is relatively low temperature. The pressures are normally self-generated in the closed vessels, which is energy-economizing. This unique characteristic has brought the benefits of reducing energy consumption and process expenditure. Also, the solvent and catalyst used during the whole HTC process is usually water, which is undoubted a nontoxic and cheap reagent. HTC treatment normally includes processes such as hydrolysis, dehydration, fragmentation, polymerization, condensation and aromatization [85]. The as-synthesized CDs normally possess rich oxygen-functionalities on the surface, thus providing good solubility in the water [86]. Currently a broad range of biomass can be transformed into CDs via HTC technique, some of which are summarized in Table 1 .

Microwave is electromagnetic wave with the frequency ranging from 300 MHz to 300 GHz and the wavelength ranging between 1 m to 1 mm. During the process of microwave irradiation, the reaction depends on the interactions between the electromagnetic wave and electric dipole of the molecules. Since the microwave irradiation is rapid volumetric heating without the heat conduction process, this leads to superiorities such as short reaction time, low energy consumption, uniform heating, high yields, simple operation and high efficiency [117]. Carbohydrates have frequently been used as carbon sources during microwave irradiation to synthesize CDs. In 2009, Zhu et al. successfully synthesized CDs from glucose and fructose by simply heating the aqueous solution which contained saccharide and poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG-200) in a 500 W microwave platform [118]. As the reaction time extended from 2 to 10 min, the solution changed from colorless to dark brown, indicating the formation of CDs. PEG-200 was utilized as the passivating agent to increase the QY of CDs. It was shown that CDs without PEG-200 passivation expressed a poor luminescence with irregular pattern in the PL spectra, whereas the CDs after PEG-200 passivation exhibited the typical PL peak. In another study, highly fluorescent crystalline CDs with size in between 3 and 10 nm were synthesized by heating sucrose/phosphoric acid solution in a microwave oven at 100 W for 220 s. To further improve the fluorescent performance of the resulting CDs, post-functionalization treatment was applied by grating certain fluorescein such as rhodamine B and α-naphthylamine on the surface of CDs [119]. Ghosh et al. directly used PEG-200 both as a carbon source and passivating agent to obtain fluorescent CDs by irradiation of the aqueous PEG-200 solution in a 900 W domestic microwave oven for 10 min [120]. QY of the CDs derived solely from PEG-200 was calculated to be 16%, which was higher than that of CDs in the above-mentioned Zhu's work. In addition, feedstock such as tomato [121], lactose [122], amino [123], ascorbic acid and l-Lysine [124] can also be utilized as green carbon sources for the synthesis of CDs by microwave irradiation. Although microwave irradiation method owns the particular merits of low requirement on equipment and short synthesis period, it is generally difficult to obtain narrow particle size distribution [125]. Another approach to synthesize CDs by virtue of microwave irradiation is called microwave-assisted HTC, where the reaction is performed in closed vials with some pressure. Integrating hydrothermal processing with microwave heating makes the CDs synthesize process faster, greener and more efficient. Water as a cheap, clean and renewable solvent can be superheated in closed vessels to effectively transform biomass into carbon materials because superheated water has a higher concentration of H3O+ ion as compared to the normal liquid water, which is capable to acid-catalyze the carbonization reaction [126]. Also, water is an ideal absorber of microwave energy, thus being preferred in the microwave-assisted HTC process [127]. The obtained CDs normally demonstrated more homogeneity in size and composition. For example, the peanut-shell derived CDs were synthesized by mixing peanut powder and DI water in a sealed Teflon vessel, followed by heating in a microwave workstation at 180 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, CDs were collected by dialyzing the supernatant solution against DI water and freeze-drying process. The particle size of the obtained CDs demonstrated a homogeneous size distribution, ranging between 2 and 6 nm. The atomic percent of nitrogen was 14.1% [118]. In a microwave-assisted HTC method to synthesize CDs developed by our group, vessels containing carbohydrates and water/sulfuric acid (10:1 or 100:1 w/w) were closed airtight and placed into the microwave platform at 200 °C for 2 h. Thereafter the carbonaceous spheres were exfoliated and oxidized in the nitric acid solution to obtain the oxygen-rich nGO-CDs with the C/O ratio around 1.6, demonstrating higher amount of oxygen functionalities compared with other studies, probably due to the separate oxidation step [81].

Ultrasound refers to the inaudible sound wave with frequency higher than 20 kHz, above the upper limit of human hearing. It can be spread via any elastic medium including water, gas-saturated water and slurry. Compared with audible sound, the ultrasound possesses much higher energy which can generate the ultrasonic cavitation effect, thus directly leading to high-speed collision of liquid flow and high shearing force of fluid, and this in turn is able to pyrolyze, combust or break the bonds of organic molecules in the aqueous environment [128]. Currently, applying ultrasonic treatment to synthesize CDs has become attractive due to various benefits including low energy demand, environmental friendliness, cost-efficiency, and compact on-site devices. Park et al. [129] have developed a simple and eco-friendly ultrasonic approach for the large-scale synthesis of water-soluble CDs from the raw food wastes. Approximately 120 g of CDs/100 kg of food waste could be produced by simply ultrasonicating food wastes/ethanol mixture for 45 min at room temperature, with the frequency of 40 kHz. The obtained CDs exhibited excellent photostability and low cytotoxicity (<2 mg/mL), which indicates potential for bio-imaging applications. Ma et al. [53] synthesized water-soluble N-doped CDs by a facile one-pot ultrasonic reaction between glucose and ammonium hydroxide. The obtained CDs demonstrated strong luminescence in the visible-to-near infrared range, and displayed clear up-conversion of photoluminescence properties. Furthermore, the CDs exhibited photocatalytic property for the photodegradation of methyl orange under visible light. The oligomer-polyamide derived CDs which emitted white fluorescence were synthesized by Dang et al. via a one-step ultrasonic treatment at room temperature for 3 h [130]. The QY of the CDs could be further promoted from 3.3% to 28.3% with the addition of silane coupling agent as the passivating agent. The as-prepared CDs had good dispersion properties, low crystallinity and rich functional groups at the surface.

Ozone and hydrogen peroxide are considered as green oxidants that usually generate non-toxic and pollution-free reduction products. Ce et al. [131] successfully converted cheap long-flame coal to water-dispersible CDs (2–9 nm) via one-step ozone oxidation process. It is also worth noting that this method can effectively generate CDs at room temperature without external energy supply. The obtained CDs demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity to the phenol molecule in the aqueous solution with the detection limit of 0.076 μM due to the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs. Subsequently, Zhang et al. [52] developed a green and simple chemical oxidation method by using hydrogen peroxide as the oxidizing agent to transform coke into CDs during 3 h treatment at 40 °C. The excess amount of H2O2 could be directly removed by employing the facile solution boiling strategy without the addition of base to neutralize. The coke-based CDs with the average diameter of 6.5 nm contained hydroxyl, carboxyl and ether groups. Under UV light illumination, the obtained CDs emitted blue fluorescence and demonstrated excitation-dependent PL emission, which acted as a proof of concept for assembling white LED device.

Self-exothermic synthesis is a simple and energy-saving approach to produce CDs at room temperature with no requirement for external energy supply. During the self-exothermic synthesis process, the small organic molecules can be rapidly carbonized by the heat generated from the exothermic reaction rather than with the help of external heating equipment. Normally, the as-prepared CDs emit stable and strong fluorescence, showing the promise in the application of cellular imaging and visual analysis. Fang et al. [132] developed an ingenious strategy for the fabrication of cross-linked hollow CDs with green fluorescence by simply mixing the glacial acetic acid (AC), water and phosphoric anhydride (P2O5) at room temperature. A large amount of heat can be rapidly released when excess P2O5 reacted with water, thus producing polyphosphoric acid (PPA) with the function of promoting carbonization reaction at low temperature. At the presence of PPA, AC can be further carbonized to form CDs by taking advantage of the released heat from the self-exothermic reaction (H2O/P2O5) at room temperature. Huang's group [133] prepared bright green-emissive CQDs at room temperature by simply mixing hydroquinone and H2O2, with further addition of ethylenediamine (EDA) as the catalyst for H2O2 decomposition. The decomposition of H2O2 favors the formation of p-benzoquinone, which is the oxidation product of hydroquinone. The extensive amount of heat released from H2O2 decomposition can effectively promote the Schiff base condensation reaction between p-benzoquinone with EDA, and simultaneously the carbonization also take place, leading to the formation of CQD. In another study, this group synthesized single-layered graphene quantum dots at room temperature via mixing hydroquinone, H2O and triethylenetetramine (TETA) [134]. Here, TETA with the same function as EDA was selected to trigger the decomposition of H2O2. The TETA-catalyzed CDs had similar diameter (around 4 nm), with the EDA-catalyzed CDs, but a remarkably lowerQY value. The EDA-catalyzed CDs displayed QY value of 26.4%, while the TETA-catalyzed CDs displayed QY value of 2.3%. Lei et al. [135] successfully synthesized CDs at room temperature via a facile one-pot strategy by mixing 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and citric acid (CA) at room temperature. The crosslinking conjugation between CA and EDC enabled “self-exothermic reaction” which can be attributed to the exothermic cross-linking reaction, thus further facilitating the formation of CDs. The chelation effect between Fe3+and -OH, -COOH, and -NH2 on the surface of CDs led to the electron-transfer induced fluorescence quenching, which provided excellent sensing capability towards Fe3+. Fu's group [136] artfully developed a strategy for the synthesis of highly fluorescent N, S-co-doped CDs at room temperature by reacting tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) with Na2S2O8. The large amount of heat released from Na2S2O8-triggered oxidation of TEPA could effectively promote the spontaneous polymerization and carbonization of TEPA precursors themselves as well as the in situ co-doping of sulfur and nitrogen. The N, S-co-doped CDs acted as nanoprobes for quantitative determination of Fe3+ in the human serum samples.

CDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles, with the particle size below 10 nm and the lattice spacing ranging between 0.18 and 0.24 nm [137]. Generally, CDs consist of amorphous or nanocrystalline cores with sp2 clusters and the attachment of oxygen functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and aldehyde groups on the surface of edge planes [138]. In order to endow new properties, CDs have been modified by the heteroatom doping process, in which some carbon atoms in the graphitic structure are replaced by the heteroatoms including N, S, P elements. The morphological information of CDs can be obtained from the TEM and AFM images. As shown in Fig. 2 , the average particle size information can be collected from TEM and the height of CDs can be measured from AFM [139,140]. Most biomass-derived CDs demonstrate sizes less than 10 nm, such as the CDs derived from wax gourd [8],pomegranate [89], bee pollen [55] and grass [94], which exhibited size ranges of 4.5–5.2, 2.5–6.5, 1.0–2.0, and 3.0–5.0 nm, respectively. However, there are exceptions of CDs demonstrating larger size range. For instance, CDs derived from silkworm chrysalis [141], oatmeal [96], and kitchen waste [142] had a size range of 19.0 nm, 20.0–40.0 nm and 50.0 nm respectively. Further structural information can be obtained by combination of various techniques. Raman spectroscopy, XRD, and HRTEM can be utilized to infer the morphology and crystalline properties of CDs. In the Raman spectra, the as-synthesized CDs have two broad bands at 1365 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1, which correspond to the sp3 defect (disordered D band) and the in-plane vibration of the sp2 carbon (crystalline G band) [137]. The relative intensity (ID/IG) is often used to evaluate the degree of crystallinity in the core. The lattice spacing of the CDs corresponding to the different diffraction planes can be measured from the HRTEM images [139]. The amorphous characteristics of biomass derived CDs can be revealed by the XRD spectra. Normally CDs demonstrate a broad diffraction peak in the 2θ range of 20–25°, which is ascribed to the graphitic plane [70,143–146]. The surface functional groups and elemental composition of CDs can be determined with the assistance of FTIR and XPS. In the FTIR spectrum of a typical biomass-derived CDs, for example the chitosan-derived CDs, the characteristic peaks are exhibited at 1218 cm−1, 1546 cm−1, 1720 cm−1, 3500–3000 cm−1, indicating the C-O-C stretching, sp2 CC stretching, C=O stretching, and –OH stretching respectively [16]. In the XPS C 1 s spectrum, the carbon bonds such as C-O, and sp2 C=C and sp3 C-C can be recognized as the peaks appearing at 286.6 eV and 284.6 eV respectively [81]. Also, the content of each group on the surface can be quantitatively analyzed via the XPS spectrum. The presence of COOH, C-OH, C=O and C-O-C peaks reveal the functionalization of CD with carboxylic, hydroxyl, carbonyl and epoxy groups, which endows CDs with good solubility and stability in the polar solutions, and provides potentials for further surface modification.

Nanocomposite materials are multicomponent materials consisting of different phase domains where at least one of the phase domains is a continuous phase and at least one phase has at least one dimension that is less than 100 nm. For the CD/polymer nanocomposites, typically the polymer is considered as the continuous phase, and the CDs are considered as the nano-fillers or property enhancers. Typically, CDs can add or improve the properties or functions of the materials already in use for specific applications. Accordingly, the core structure and properties of the CD/polymer nanocomposites are determined by the polymer matrix which can be thermoplastic (linear or branched) or thermoset (crosslinked polymer), and CDs can be either covalently-attached to or non-covalently blended with the polymer matrix. The polymer matrix is selected based on the application. The chemical structure of the polymer or the thermoset resin determines the strength of the secondary interactions between the polymer and CDs and/or the possibility to covalently attach CDs, which will further influence the dispersion of CDs and the preparation cost. The current approaches to construct CD/polymer nanocomposites can be classified into physical blending, chemical grafting, and in-situ growth.

Physical blending approach is a facile method to prepare CD/polymer nanocomposite materials and involves mixing tiny amounts of CDs with the polymer matrix either by solution blending or melt processing. The formation of the nanocomposite is principally ascribed to the noncovalent interactions such as hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions or π-π interactions between the polymer and CDs [25,147–148]. Hydrogen bonding is an intermolecular interaction between a partially positively charged H atom in one molecule and a partially negatively charged N, O or F atom in a nearby molecule. The formation of hydrogen bonds take place mainly via the electrostatic attraction which is weaker than covalent bond or ionic bond, but significantly stronger than van der Waals' forces between the non-polar molecules. The electrostatic interactions are related with the attractive or repulsive forces between charged molecules. When using this method to prepare CD/polymer composites, the attractive interactions are the dominant driving force for obtaining good-quality nanocomposites with well-distributed CDs. Normally CDs exhibit negative surface charges due to the presence of carboxylic group, thus tending to form interactions with polymers with positive charge. Recently, our group utilized physical blending method for the CD/polymer nanocomposite fabrication. For example, incorporation of coffee ground-derived nGO-CDs in the polylactide (PLA) or stereocomplex PLA films allowed the creation of favorable interfacial interactions, benefitting the exfoliation and dispersion of nGO-CDs in the matrix (Fig. 3a and b) [149,150]. The well-dispersed nGO-CDs permitted high transmission to visible light and endowed the films with excellent UV-shielding function in the full UV wavelength region at only 0.5–1 wt% of nGO-CDs. (Fig. 3c) nGO-CDs remarkably facilitated the PLA crystallization (Fig. 3d), which led to the promoted thermomechanical properties and resistance to oxygen permeation. Although nGO-CDs could induce bioactivity on the surface of PCL, the mechanical properties of PCL were compromised due to the poor compatibility of the hydrophilic nGO-CDs with hydrophobic PCL. Hence, to reduce the hydrophilicity r-nGO-CDs were generated by using caffeic acid as the reducing agent. The produced r-nGO-CDs could be homogenously incorporated into PCL matrix by solution casting, extrusion and compression molding steps (Fig. 3e) [151]. After the reduction step the bioactivity was retained and r-nGO-CDs could facilitate the hydroxyapatite mineralization on the PCL surface (Fig. 3f). Furthermore, the storage modulus and creep resistance of the PCL films were remarkably improved (Fig. 3g and h). In another study, starch-based electrospun nanofibrous scaffolds were fabricated by utilizing the starch-derived nGO-CDs as property enhancers (Fig. 3i) [12]. The addition of nGO-CDs improved the electrospinnability of starch and promoted the thermal stability of the fibers. It was also revealed that the diameter distribution of the fibers was narrowed by the nGO-CDs addition (Fig. 3j and k). Tarciane et at. [152] fabricated an oxidized CD/chitosan multilayer film by using a layer-by-layer self-assembly strategy. The thickness and morphology of the films could be easily regulated by controlling the ionic strength and pH value of the CDs and the chitosan solutions. π-π interactions can exist between two adjacent molecules, both of which possess aromatic groups. Since CDs including GQDs are generally rich in conjugated sp2 domains, they can form π-π interactions with polymers possessing the aromatic groups such as polystyrene and polypyrrole or the polymers functionalized with p-orbital-rich groups such as phenyl, pyrene, and dipyrene [148]. The physical blending method has the merits of simple operation and low cost and especially for the melt-processing approach is also easy to industrialize. However, the agglomeration problem ascribed to the self-π-π stacking interactions between CD nanoparticles can impede nonhomogeneous dispersion of CDs in the polymer matrix, resulting in the poor mechanical strength and unstable optical properties. In addition, since only noncovalent interactions exit between CDs and polymer matrix, the migration of CDs from the composite material could also take place [153].

The existence of abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs expands the possibility of chemical modifications to form covalent bonds with polymers via for example esterification [154], etherification epoxidation [155], methacrylation/acrylation and acylation [156]. Compared with physical blending approach, chemical grafting of CDs to polymer matrix is superior in the aspects of promoting mechanical strength and maintaining the properties over the time due to the covalent bonds between the polymer chains and CDs [157]. Another advantage of chemical grafting lies in the formation of composites with more uniform properties. In recent years, our group has successfully prepared a series of CD/biopolymer composite materials by covalent functionalization of biopolymers or polymers with bio-derived CDs. For instance, Erdal et al. [158] grafted cellulose-derived nGO-CDs on the surface of porous polycaprolactone (PCL) scaffolds via a mild reaction between the epoxy functionalities of the nGO-CDs and the free amine groups of aminolyzed PCL scaffold surface (Fig. 4a). The covalent attachment of nGO-CDs on the surface of PCL scaffolds could in best cases lead to a 50% improvement in the compressive strength. Also, the nGO-CD-grafted scaffold exhibited significantly enhanced bioactivity as evidenced by the formation of mineral precipitates after the incubation of the scaffolds in simulated body fluid. In another study, Wu et al. [34] fabricated fully starch-derived bioactive 3D porous scaffolds by firstly attaching starch-based nGO-CDs to the starch chains via esterification reaction to prepare nGO-CD-reinforced starch (S-nGOCDs), followed by freeze-drying the solutions containing S-nGOCDs and non-modified starch in different ratios (Fig. 4b). nGO-CDs as bioactive nanofillers, functioned as effective anchoring sites inducing the calcium phosphate (CaP) mineralization in the simulated body fluid. It was shown that the porous scaffolds with 50 wt% of S-nGOCDs induced the largest amount of hydroxyapatite, with the ideal Ca/P ratio of 1.67, indicating potential promise for bone/cartilage tissue engineering. In addition to the above-mentioned approaches of directly attaching CDs to the polymer chains or modified polymer matrix, there are other methods which demand the surface functionalization of the CDs as well. Feng et al. [159] grafted photo-curable methacrylate groups on chitosan-derived nGO-CDs to synthesize methacrylated-nGO-CDs (M-nGO-CDs). M-nGO-CDs and methacrylated-chitosan (M-CS) were then utilized to build up photocued M-nGO-CD/M-CS nanocomposite hydrogels under exposure to the visible light (Fig. 4d). It is interesting to notice that the storage modulus of the fabricated composite hydrogels increased compared with the reference sample which only contained M-CS matrix, whereas the addition of neat nGO-CDs to the M-CS matrix via physical blending method led to decreased storage modulus. This can be explained by the screening effect of nGO-CDs during the photopolymerization process of the M-CS matrix. Similar results were obtained during photocuring of methacrylated carboxymethyl cellulose and M-nGO-CDs (Fig. 4e) [160]. Gustavsson et al. [161] fabricated thermoplastic “all-cellulose” oxidized carbonized cellulose (OCC)/cellulose composite film via covalent functionalization of cellulose acetate (CA) by cellulose-based OCC prepared by microwave-assisted HTC of cellulose and paper tissues (Fig. 4c). The OCC resembled nGO-CDs but were significantly larger on lateral diameter. Before reaction with CA, the obtained OCCs were surface modified to acyl-chlorinated OCCs (OCC-Cl) by reaction with thionyl chloride to promote the reactivity towards the remaining -OH groups in CA. In comparison to films prepared from neat CA or CA with physically blended OCCs, the films with covalently attached OCCs demonstrated great improvement of optical and mechanical properties. Ghosh et al. [162] prepared CD/polyaniline (PANI) nanocomposites by chemically coupling N,N-bis(4′-aminophenyl)-1,4-quinonediimine (APQD) moieties on the surface of candle-soot derived CDs (Fig. 4f). The aim was to direct the oxidative polymerization of aniline to form PANI chains at the presence of APQD, hence the PANI chains were grafted from the CDs surface. The covalently attached CD/PANI composites showed excellent electrochemical performance, with a significantly higher specific capacitance of 972 F/g with 90% retention after 2000 cycles, in comparison to only 482 F/g for the non-covalently attached CD/PANI composites.

Currently, the mainstream approach to construct CD/polymer nanocomposites is via physical blending method, however the secondary intermolecular interaction between CDs and polymer matrix can be relatively weak and unstable. Although the chemical grafting method can strongly anchor CDs on the polymer matrix due to the formation of covalent bonds, its shortcomings lie in the involvement of multiple preparation steps, complicated reaction processes and in many cases utilization of toxic organic reagents and solvents. In comparison, in-situ growth of CDs within the polymer matrix is a facile and green process, and the bonding type between the CDs and polymer includes both chemical and physical interactions, thus the firmness of the attachment can be better ensured. Normally, in-situ growth strategy can be achieved by applying one-pot thermal treatment including hydrothermal, pyrolysis or low-temperature heating on the mixture containing CD precursors and polymer solution [163,164]. The formation of CDs and the building up of the composite materials are not isolated procedures, but rather they take place simultaneously. Also, CDs will not be passively immobilized into the polymer matrix, which has potential limitations such as poor compatibility of the two components leading to the undesirable heterogeneous dispersion of CDs and unevenly-distributed properties. Instead, the CDs will nucleate and grow on the active sites of the polymer straightforwardly, thus generating a highly stable and homogenous system [165]. For example, Kumar et al. [166] synthesized CD-embedded magnetic fluorescent nanocomposites by in-situ growth of the CDs on the surface of carboxymethyl chitosan-wrapped Fe3O4 nanoparticles through one-pot hydrothermal strategy. At a temperature of 140 °C, CDs originated from partial carbonization of carboxymethyl chitosan were in-situ attached on the surface of carboxymethyl chitosan to form CD@carboxymethyl chitosan matrix, and further this matrix were utilized to wrap Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 5a). For the application of cancer (HeLa) cells imaging, the obtained CD@carboxymethyl chitosan/Fe3O4 nanocomposite particles were conjugated with folic acid, thus being endowed with specific-targeting property towards cancer cells which possess a number of folate receptors. The appearance of green florescence from the fluorescent microscopic image indicated the cellular internalization process of nanoparticles on HeLa cell (Fig. 5b). Fernandes et al. [163] disclosed a solvent-free, time and cost-effective strategy to synthesize a range of CD/polymer nanocomposites via in-situ approach which was compatible with standard melt-processing technologies routinely employed by the industry. The strategy relied on the pyrolysis of ethanolamine (a carbon and nitrogen-rich precursor), which was well dispersed within a variety of polymer matrixes (polyethylene, polypropylene and polyethylene glycol). This process in-situ generated the CD-embedded fluorescent nanocomposites without compromising the physiochemical properties of the polymeric matrixes (Fig. 5c), because the temperature applied was lower than the pyrolytic decomposition temperature of the polymers. At least 20% of the ethanolamine could be converted into CDs with a diameter range of 1–40 nm. Lian et al. [165] prepared stretchable and mechanically stable multi-color CD/polyurethane (CD/PU) films via in-situ condensation and aging of carboxylated PU in the presence of various CD precursors (o-phenylenediamine (o-PD), m-phenylenediamine (m-PD), and 1,2,4-triphenylamine (1,2,4-3TD). The formed CDs were well fixed and dispersed in the PU matrix due to the interactions of amine groups from the carbon sources with the carboxylate group of PU. As shown in Fig. 5d, the as-prepared composite films could emit diverse colors from blue to red, depending on the type of CD precursor. This might be related with graphitization, surface functionalization and particle size of CDs. The composite films also showed that more than 90% of the emission intensity could be preserved after soaking in water for 30 days, aging for up to 6 h at 100 °C and after subjection of the materials to several cycles of stretching and natural recovery (Fig. 5e). This is promising for the use in solid-state lighting applications.

CDs as the emerging family of carbon nanomaterials have shown a promising future in a broad field of applications due to the easy preparation, unique properties and diversity in structures and compositions. All these favorable characteristics, together with abundant surficial polar functionalities, have endowed CDs with great potential for further fabrication into CD/polymer nanocomposites, which can bring significant improvements in performance and a series of new functions. Recently, the applications of CD/polymer nanocomposite materials have covered diverse fields in which energy storage, environmental and biomedical domains have aroused intensive research focus. Therefore, this section will comprehensively summarize and highlight the progress of applying CD/polymer nanocomposites in the aforementioned frontier fields. Depending on the application, there are different requirements for the final properties of the CD/polymer nanocomposites. In the energy field, good electron charge transfer and ionic conductivity are essential, whereas in the environmental area, the nontoxicity and reusability of the nanocomposites plays a significant role. In biomedical applications, properties such as hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, nontoxicity and biodegradability are of importance to consider.

The rapid population growth and increasing demand of devices including electric automobiles, power backup and various electronic applications are stimulating the development of new energy storage devices such as supercapacitors and next-generation batteries. CDs are promising materials for several energy storage applications.

The distinguished advantages of supercapacitors in comparison with batteries can be ascribed as faster charge/discharge rate, longer cycle life and greater stability, thus attracting significant interest in both academia and industry [167]. An intact supercapacitor is composed of four main elements, electrode materials, current collector, separator and electrolyte, among which the electrode materials are of paramount significance as they dominantly determine the electrochemical performance of the supercapacitors [168]. On the basis of the electrode materials and the type of charge storage mechanism, the supercapacitors can be sorted as pseudocapacitors and electrical double layer capacitors (EDLCs). Although pseudocapacitors with conducting polymers as the efficient electrode materials exhibit higher energy density and higher specific capacitance than EDLCs, the downside lies in the relatively lower mechanical stability and cycle life, which further leads to a poor charge/discharge capability. On the other hand, the carbon-based EDLCs have the distinguished merits of long operational life [169]. Therefore, coupling conducting polymers with carbon materials can be a promising approach to fabricate an electrode combining the two advantages. Recently, CDs have demonstrated bright potential for integration with low-cost conducting polymers such as polypyrrole (PPy), polyaniline (PANI) and polythiophene (PTH) in the supercapacitor [170,171]. A strong interaction of CDs in the nanocomposite has been revealed to promote the electron transport and ionic motion during the supercapacitor charge/discharge process and it promotes fast redox reactions and high specific capacitance. For instance, Zhang et al. [172] has synthesized a ternary composite electrode of graphene oxide/CD/polypyrrole (GO/CD/PPy) through in-situ polymerization of GO/CDs with pyrrole under a mild condition (Fig. 6a). The CDs sandwiched between GO film and PPy layer can boost the electron transportation from PPy to GO in the ternary composite system, and reduce both the internal resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the electrode materials, which were in line with the promoted electrochemical performance such as higher specific capacitance of 576 F/g as comparison to the electrode GO/PPy of 468 F/g at 0.5 A/g (Fig. 6c), higher capacitance retention of 92.9% as comparison to GO/PPy with capacitance retention of 83.7% after 5000 cycles at a current density of 10.0 A/g (Fig. 6d), smaller Rs value of 0.73 and Rct value of 1.42 as comparison to GO/PPy with Rs value of 3.51 and Rct value of 2.39 (Fig. 6e). As a proof-of-concept for the actual application, an assembled device with five symmetric supercapacitors in series was charged for 17 s to 5.0 V at 10 A/g, which was able for illuminating the LED indicator for over 1 min (Fig. 6b). Toyoko et al. [173] has employed an in-situ chemical oxidative polymerization method to synthesize CD@PPy and CD@PANI composites by using ammonium persulfate (APS) as an oxidizing agent. CDs promoted the specific capacitance of the polymeric pseudocapacitors which was evidenced by the more than doubled specific capacitance values of CD@PPy (676 F/g) and CD@PANI (529 F/g) at 1 A/g compared with pristine PPy (245 F/g) and PANI (222 F/g). The Coulombic efficiency of pristine PPy and PANI electrodes were measured to be 85.2% and 91.8%; whereas after 4000 cycles, the CD@PPy and CD@PANI demonstrated efficiencies of 99.2% and 97.5% respectively. This clearly indicates that the cycle durability of the supercapacitors had been strengthened by the addition of CDs. Oskueyan et al. [174] fabricated a nanocomposite of PANI-CD/PPy-graphene and used it as the supercapacitor electrode material. It was shown the PANI-CD/PPy-graphene composite electrode with a 10 wt% of CDs displayed a specific capacitance of 396 F/g, which was significantly higher than PANI-CD and PPy-graphene electrodes with the specific capacitances of 193 and 229 F/g at 1 A/g. In another work, Oskueyan et al. [32] prepared a hybrid supercapacitor based on N,S co-doped GQD decorated CeO2/PANI composite. The ultrafine N,S-GQDs were in-situ grown on CeO2 nanoparticles by hydrothermal process. It was revealed that the incorporation of S and N elements in the GQD could greatly enhance the contact area with CeO2 and provide larger active surface, which in return displayed positive effects on the electrochemical properties of the CeO2/PANI composite electrodes. In comparison with the specific capacitance of 115 F/g for CeO2/PANI, N,S-GQD@CeO2/PANI exhibited the specific capacitance of 189 F/g at current density of 1 A/g. Except for the aforementioned works, other similar studies carried out by Zhang [172], Liu [175], Lu [176], Zhou [177] and Vandana [178] also proved that CDs can improve the electrochemical performance of polymer-based supercapacitors. These studies are summarized in Table 2 .

Another important role of CDs in the build-up of CD/polymer nanocomposite electrodes is their participation in the conductive polymer synthesis. Normally strong oxidizing agents such as iron chloride, silver nitrate, potassium persulfate, ammonium persulfate, and copper are employed as initiators for the polymerization of monomers such as aniline and pyrrole; however, these initiators are toxic and not environmental-friendly [179]. Recently, it was shown that CDs are capable of producing free radicals in aqueous solution, and these free radicals may participate in the polymerization process. Moorthy et al. [180] introduced a novel one-pot synthesis of PANI by merely using CDs and UV light as initiators. Here, the CDs were synthesized from polyethylene glycol-400 (PEG-400). PANI was synthesized by placing the solution containing CDs and aniline under the UV irradiation for 1 week. Likewise, synthesis of PPy via in-situ polymerization of pyrrole in the presence of GQD suspension was developed by Wu et al. [181] It was shown that PPy flakes were covalently attached on the surface of the GQD. As the supercapacitor electrode, the GQD/PPy nanocomposite with the mass ratio of PPy to GQD at 50:1 demonstrated a specific capacitance of 485 F/g at a scan rate of 0.005 V/s. After 2000 cycles, the attenuation of the specific capacitance was only 2%.

The lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been in the focus of strong research interest, as they exhibit high working voltage, high storage capacity, long charge/discharge lifespan and environmental benignity. LIBs belong to the family of secondary batteries, in which the lithium ions deintercalate from the cathode and are embed into the anode during the discharge process and vice versa when charging [182]. Cathode, anode, electrolyte and separator are the four indispensable components determining the electrochemical performance of LIBs. In comparison to the conventional LIBs using liquid electrolyte system in which the leakage of flammable and volatile organic solvents can endow the critical safety threads such as a short circuit and even explosion, the solid polymer electrolyte (SPE) demonstrates improved safety. In addition, SPE possesses low flammability, and excellent thermal and electrochemical stability, which can broaden the operating condition window to higher temperatures and working voltages. The flexibility of SPE provide the possibility for assembling batteries in various package styles [183]. The certain mechanical strength of SPE plays a role in alleviating the volumetric expansion of active electrode [184]. Therefore, SPE has been considered for the commercialization in the new-generation energy storage devices. Due to the benefits of high dielectric constant and strong Li+ ion solvating ability, poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO)-based SPE, as a typical representative of SPEs, has been widely studied. However, the shortcomings lie in the intrinsically low ionic conductivity (10−7–10−6 S cm−1) [185]. To improve the ionic conductivity, Ma et al. [27] has prepared the CQDs via aldol reaction of acetone to form unsaturated ketone, followed by the polymerization of the ketone leading to oligomer products with extended carbon chains. These oligomer chains eventually produce CQDs via curling and intertwining induced by thermal motion (Fig. 7a). Subsequently, the CQDs with rich oxygen functionalities were incorporated into the PEO-based matrix to fabricate PEO/CQD-LiClO4 electrolytes. In this system, the well-dispersed CQDs with the diameter range of 2–3 nm provided numerous Lewis acid sites that effectively promoted the adsorption of ClO4 − in the electrolyte, which vice versa enhance the dissociation of lithium salt. At meantime, the interactions between CQDs and PEO molecules will lower the crystallinity degree of PEO and generate more amorphous region which facilitates the Li+ transfer (Fig. 7b and c), thus exhibiting high ionic conductivity of 1.39 × 10−4 S cm−1 and a high lithium-ion transference number of 0.48. Moreover, PEO/CQD-LiClO4 based battery demonstrated higher specific discharge capacities of 140, 138, 133, 121, 88 mAh g−1 at 0.5, 1, 2, 4 and 8C respectively, whereas the corresponding capacities for PEO-LiClO4 battery were 136, 118, 108, 75 and 13 mAh g−1.

Li-S battery as a promising type of lithium battery has been developed with significant progress in high specific capacity and excellent cyclic stability. However, some critical issues including low conductivity and polysulfide shuttling effect (refers to the dissolution and migration of polysulfide from cathode to anode, leading to the rapidly fading capacity) emerge when attempts are made to improve the areal sulfur loading and to increase the operation current density to meet the standards for various industrial applications [186]. To solve these problems, polyethylenimine (PEI)-functionalized CD cathodes have been introduced into the Li-S battery systems [187]. The CD/PEI composites were obtained by the one-pot carbonization of PEI at 400 °C for 40 min. Then CD/PEI was blended with sulfur (S), polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF), and acetylene black (AB) to prepare the PEI/CD@AB/S/PVDF cathode. It was revealed that CDs with high specific surface area and abundant functional sites are capable of adsorbing and localizing polysulfide on the cathode and promoting Li+ conductivity at the cathode-electrode interface. In turn, at a high current density of 8 mA cm−2, this battery delivered a reversible areal capacity of 3.3 mAh cm−2 with merely 0.07% capacity decay after 400 cycles at 6.6 mg of sulfur loading. To solve the issue of polysulfide shuttling effect in Li-S battery, apart from engineering the electrode materials especially focusing on the composition and structure, another solution could be focused on the separator design which is facile and cost-effective. For example, Pang et al. [188] has designed a modified separator by coating ultralight multiwall carbon nanotube/N-doped carbon quantum dot (MWCNTs/NCQDs) composites on the surface of the polypropylene (PP) separator (Fig. 8a). NCQDs with abundant oxygenated functional groups (such as -COOH, -OH) provided plenty of active sites for the polysulfide anion adsorption in Li-S battery through chemical bonding, thus improving certain electrochemical performance such as high initial discharge capacity of 1330.8 mA h g−1 (Fig. 8b) and excellent cyclic performance with the discharge capacity retaining at 507.9 mA h g−1 after 1000 cycles (Fig. 8c), which corresponds to the capacity fade rate as low as 0.05% per cycle.

The rapid growth of population and global industrialization have contributed to the severe water pollution, which in turn brings side effect to the ecosystem and threatens human health. Owing to the unique fluorescent properties, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, abundant functional groups, CDs have shown great potentials in the contaminant detection and remediation application. In this field, there has been an increasing number of studies centering on utilizing CD/polymer nanocomposite rather than the CDs alone due to the incomparable benefits of composite materials such as improved stability, portability, recyclability and expanded functionality. Here, the applications of CD/polymer nanocomposites in the environmental field will be divided into two main sections, fluorescent probes for the contaminants detection and adsorbents for the contaminants removal.

In recent years, CDs have been widely evaluated as fluorescent probes to quantitatively detect trace chemical contaminants in the aquatic systems with the help of the characteristic fluorescence quenching effect [166,189,190]. Behind this phenomenon lies the alteration of electron, charge or energy transmission pathways through the interactions between the contaminant molecules with the functionalities on the surface of the CDs. This leads to the significant attenuation or extinction of CDs' intrinsic fluorescence intensity. Based on this principle, several mechanisms have been proposed to construct fluorescence sensing probes, namely fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced-charge transfer (PCT), photoinduced-electron transfer (PET), aggregation induced emission quenching (AIEQ), and inner filter effect (IFE) [191]. In comparison with traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, CDs generally demonstrate large two-photo absorption area, good photostability and water solubility. In addition, CDs also display the merits of low toxicity, good biocompatibility, low-cost, simple and potentially eco-friendly preparation, thus making CDs a promising component for the design of fluorescent sensing platforms. The fluorescent probes can be built up by using the CDs alone, which has been normally achieved by dispersing the CDs in the aqueous solution to prepare the liquid-state fluorescent probe [192]. However, this has unavoidable shortcomings such as relatively low recovery efficiency and poor portability. Also, the interference of some uncertain factors including pH value and salt concentration in the real aquatic environments might destroy CDs' dispersion stability, which could lead to the aggregation of the CDs. As a result, the fluorescence self-quenching phenomenon might occur [193,194]. To resolve these problems, construction of solid-state fluorescent probes via introduction of CDs into the polymer matrix has been developed. Compared with the liquid-state fluorescent probe, a solid-state probe is easier to carry and transport, more stable and compatible with small-footprint devices. Another distinct advantage of a solid-state probe lies in the generally higher recovery efficiency due to the more convenient washing step, thus being endowed with the reversible response to the analytes [195]. Moreover, CDs can be well-dispersed in correctly chosen polymer matrix by the interaction with polymers' active sites through secondary interactions or through covalent bonding, which can effectively reduce the agglomeration problem, thereby promoting the analysis stability and accuracy. Beyond this, the presence of a polymer has been evidenced to promote PL intensity and QYs of CDs [31,196], with plausible explanations as following: (1) the agglomeration of CDs could be effectively inhibited owing to the physiochemical interactional between CDs and polymer; (2) according to the assumptions that the photoluminescence originates from the radiative recombination between the excited electron located at the surface energy trap and the holes, and the surface states of CDs can visibly influence their PL intensity. The surface of CDs can be indirectly passivated when CDs are embedded into the polymers. This leads to the effective suppression of the non-radiative transition of electrons, thereby improving the PL intensity [197]. At present, CDs have been introduced into the polymeric matrixes to fabricate different types of fluorescent sensors such as membranes [198], hydrogels [190], aerogels [199], test paper [200], and electrospun nanofibrous films [201] with remarkable function in the detection of heavy metals and organic contaminants (Table 3 ). For example, CDs derived from hydrothermal carbonization of citric acid and ethylenediamine were grafted with carboxylated cellulose nanofibrils via condensation reaction, followed by being incorporated into a lignin-based hydrogel to fabricate a solid fluorescent probe with a QY of 7.3% for the detection of heavy metal ion (Fig. 9a) [202]. The as-fabricated hydrogel probe demonstrated a visible and complete fluorescence quenching effect towards Cr6+, whereas in the presence of other metals such as Zn2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Cu2+, the hydrogel displayed incomplete fluorescence quenching effect (Fig. 9b). This illustrated specific sensitivity towards Cr6+ over other metal ions. The detection limit of Cr6+ was calculated to be 11.2 mg/L at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (Fig. 9c). The florescent S, N co-doped CDs originating from methyl blue were dispersed in PVA matrix to prepare PVA/CD films, which could act as a label-free nanoprobe for H2S detection with a detection limit of 46.8 nM (Fig. 9d and e) [203]. It is worth noticing that the as-prepared nanocomposite films exhibited dual-mode florescence properties (Fig. 9f). Under the alkaline condition, the film displayed bright blue fluorescence at 365 nm and 245 nm UV excitations; whereas under the acidic condition, the film displayed green fluorescence at 365 nm and blue fluorescence at 245 nm separately, indicating great potential as encryption material. Amino-modified cellulose membranes containing pyrene‑boronic acid (PyB)-grafted CDs were prepared for the detection of fluoride anions in water [198]. The as-prepared sensor showed a high sensitivity to F−. The response was linear for F− concentrations over a range from 0 to 200 μM, with a low detection limit of 5.9 × 10−5 M.

Different wastewater purification technologies such as membrane separation, adsorption, ion exchange, chemical precipitation, advanced oxidation process (AOPs), electrochemical methods, and biochemical processes have been developed to remove toxic contaminants from water. However, the majority of the techniques are associated with the disadvantages of complicated treatments, high cost, low reusability, and ineffectiveness at high concentrations. Among the aforementioned technologies, adsorption stands out due to unique merits such as simplicity in the process design, low operational cost, lower consumption of chemicals, high removal efficiency, recyclability and other economical features [206]. The basic principle behind adsorption is the transfer of detrimental solutes from the liquid phase to the surface of the solid adsorbents through intermolecular interactions [196]. The adsorbents are normally required to possess high specific surface areas, certain porosity, and large number of active sites. Recently, it has been reported that the two-dimensional graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets exhibited excellent adsorption performance towards many organic contaminants [207], such as dyes [208], pesticides [209], antibiotics [210], PAHs and their derivatives [211]. This is explained by high aspect ratio, the presence of delocalized π-electrons in the core and oxygen functionalities at the edges. In comparison with two-dimensional graphene or GO nanosheets, CDs and particularly GQD or GOQD with zero-dimensional size have been proven to have even higher specific surface area [81]. They can also be even richer in oxygen functionalities, which has aroused great research interest in the area of contaminant adsorption. Unlike graphene or GO, which have been utilized as they are, GQD or GOQD-based adsorbents were mostly assembled by the combination of GQD or GOQD with polymer matrix. Apart from the enhanced adsorption performance of the contaminants by the addition of GOQD or GQD in the polymer matrix, GOQD or GQD also boosted certain properties of the materials. Our group has carried out a series of studies in this area. For example, nGO-CDs produced by the subsequent carbonization-oxidation of chitosan (CS) and cellulose could promote the diclofenac sodium (DCF) adsorption capacity of chitosan hydrogels [16,81]. The robust three-dimensional crosslinked nGO-CD/CS hydrogel network demonstrated nearly 100% removal of DCF after only 5 h (Fig. 10b), which can be explained by the synergistic effect of electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bonding, and π-π stacking. Meanwhile, nGO-CDs could catalyze the chitosan hydrogel crosslinking reaction by genipin (Fig. 10a), thus generating composite hydrogels with improved mechanical strength (Fig. 10c) [16]. We also developed a facile one-pot spray-drying process for fabrication and in-situ crosslinking of nGO-CD/CS microspheres (Fig. 10d) [14]. The microspheres were proven as powerful adsorbents for the removal of methyl orange (MO) and DCF (Fig. 10f and g), with the adsorption efficiency reaching 99% and 100% respectively. By regulating the amount of nGO-CDs, the surface morphology, size and zeta potential of the composite microspheres could be controlled (Fig. 10e). In another case, the negatively-charged nGO-CDs were electrostatically coated on the surface of cationized cellulose filters (Fig. 10h and i), which demonstrated efficient adsorption capacity towards methylene blue (MB) [212]. Also, the attachment of nGO-CDs on the filter surface endowed the filter with another important function enabling rapid mass spectrometric analysis of small-molecular contaminants (<500 Da) directly on the filter surface through surface-assisted laser desorption ionization-mass spectrometry (SALDI-MS) analysis (Fig. 10j). Beyond GQD or GOQD, other types of CDs have also gained popularity in the adsorbent fabrication (Table 4 ). As an example, amine-rich CDs were anchored on the polyacrylonitrile fiber sheets via hydrothermal treatment to construct a highly recyclable adsorbent with high selectivity in the MO removal [213]. Exploring state-of-art methods to improve material's adsorption efficiency and capacity towards contaminants is of significant value; however, another key issue related to the recyclability and regeneration of the adsorbents should not be overlooked. Currently, the commonly-used strategy for the adsorbent regeneration is via elution of the contaminants from the adsorbents by consuming large amounts of acids or alkali, which may bring the risk of secondary pollution [214–217]. In comparison, achieving the adsorbent recovery by photocatalytic degradation of the contaminants adsorbed on the adsorbents under the light irradiation could be an energy-saving and environmentally-friendly strategy. As an example, a polymeric hydrogel consisting of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) cross-linked by CDs derived from citric acid and cysteamine was created and possessed a dual function of adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of both cationic and anionic dyes [218]. This was shown as within the first 30 min of being exposed to sunlight, Crystal Violet (>90%), Maclachite Green (>80%) were degraded to a degree of over 90%, and 80% respectively (Fig. 10k and10l). This can be ascribed to the unique Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) generating capability of the CDs under sunlight. Ibarbia et al. [219] incorporated GQD into the poly[2-(Methacryloyloxy)ethyl]trimethylammonium]-co-(3-Sulfopropylmethacrylate) matrix to prepare a GQD-embedded hydrogel displaying remarkable photocatalytic degradation ability towards rhodamine B (RhB) upon exposure to the visible light (Fig. 10m). It was interesting to notice that the as-prepared hydrogel could retain stable photocatalytic activity after being stored wet for several months as well as after experiencing a drying-rewetting process.

It has been highly motivating in utilizing CDs for the replacement of toxic inorganic quantum dots (QDs) for bioimaging [222]. The optical properties of CDs are advantageous with regards to broad excitation spectra, variable emission spectra and high photostability. In addition, they exhibit generally good biocompatibility and low toxicity. Other important properties of CDs and polymers have further enabled development of smart composite materials with new or enhanced properties, including improved mechanical properties of polymers.

It is an incentive many times to preserve the PL properties of CDs when incorporated in the polymer matrix for biomedical applications. The PL property of CD/polymer composites have as for instances enabled sensing biomolecules and proteins, and transfecting biopolymers with simultaneous imaging. The sensing of biomolecules such as glucose is of high value for the management of diabetes. A glucose imprinted poly (N-isopropylacrylamide-acrylamide-vinylphenylboronic acid) copolymer microgel was produced that assisted conversion of biochemical signals to optical signals with the assistance of CDs (Fig. 11a) [223]. Depending on the glucose concentration the microgels would shrink and swell, which led to quenching and recovering of the fluorescence signals. The sensing of other small biomolecules and proteins have further been developed by bioelectrodes based on CD/polymer composites [224,225]. In these type of systems the fluorescence properties of CDs were not a requirement, but the addition of CD could improve the interaction with the sensing molecule and electron transfer between sensing interface and electrode, respectively. Sensing of dopamine was demonstrated with Au@CD chitosan composite modified glassy electrode [226]. CDs enable the interaction with dopamine due to their negative charges and Au provided for better conductivity. The Au@CD chitosan composite films could further suppress background current from ascorbic acid and uric acid.

Assessment of overexpression of the protein histone can be linked to several serious diseases such as dengue. For fluorimetric detection of histone, a quarternized CD/deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) nano-biohybrid was developed [227]. In the presence of histone, DNA would preferably interact with the histone instead of the quarternized CDs. As a consequence, the fluorescence signal of quarternized CDs increased, which enabled the detection of histone. Other smart sensing systems utilizing CDs have further been employed [228]. The imprinted polymer based on the thermosensitive polypropylacrylamide (PNIPAM) with silica capped CDs were incorporated with nonpeptides for recognizing the protein Cytochrome C. PNIPAM underwent a swelling behavior above the lower critical solution temperature (LCST ~32 °C), which was utilized for recognizing and releasing the cytochrome c. Below LCST, PNIPAM displayed higher fluorescence signal and behaved as a random hydrophilic coil with optimal conditions for rebinding of the protein.

The design of multifunctional nano-carriers has shown promises for gene therapy and as a diagnostic tool. Organic dyes and inorganic QDs can be used to label polymeric vectors and plasmid DNA but have shown instability and health concerns, respectively. CDs capped with PEI network enabled a higher transfection of plasmid DNA into COS-7 and HepG2 cells compared with PEI alone [229]. Intriguingly, the mentioned hydrogel showed simultaneous imaging capability with varying excitation wavelength that held promises for in vivo imaging with near infra-red emissions. Real-time monitoring of gene delivery was further displayed by Au/CD particles complexed with PEI and non-labeled plasmid DNA [230]. A high salt concentration enabled the dissociation of the system with recoveree of the fluorescence signal.

CDs with hydrophilic surface character are a prerequisite for fabrication of hydrogels in combination with hydrophilic polymers. The hydrophilic functionalities of CDs are typically hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxide functionalities, which also serve for the generally good water dispersibility and low cytotoxicity. The functionalities of CDs may be tuned to some extent by using different precursors and processing methods [231]. Post-treatments have further shown useful for altering the oxygen content of CDs [71]. Hydrogels are highly sought for as soft materials in biomedical applications, but the mechanical properties are many times too poor for the intended usage. By utilizing CDs in modification of the chemically crosslinked hydrogel network of polyacrylamide (PAM) provided advantageous mechanical properties [232]. Here, the unzipping of physical crosslinks between PAM and CDs created a highly stretchable hydrogel with the elongation of 3700%, while the high fracture strength of 166 kPa was retained (Fig. 11b). Another composite hydrogel network, consisting of polyacrylamide exhibited blue fluorescence with addition of CDs, which also improved the mechanical performance [233]. For instance, the addition of 0.67 wt% of CDs increased the strength from 112 kPa to 161 kPa and the elongation from 676% to 842% of the hydrogel.

CD incorporated hydrogels are promising for thermo-responsive drug delivery with simultaneous bioimaging, as CDs are well-known to be able to hold drugs with help of intermolecular forces and give rise to PL under UV/visible light. CDs were disclosed in a PAM network in presence of the thermoresponsive polymer PNIPAM to produce smart hydrogels that displayed a blue color under UV-light at room temperature (Figure 11c1 and 11c2) [231]. The PL intensity of hydrogel demonstrated a temperature-dependent behavior (Figure 11c3) and this temperature-dependent PL intensity was highly reversible (Figure 11c5). Above the lower critical solution temperature (LCST), PNIPAM gave rise to a collapsed system and aggregated CDs, which decreased the intensity of PL (Figure 11c4). Same principle with changeable PL was successfully adapted in a covalent CD@PNIPAM network at a wide range of CD concentrations [234]. Furthermore, a target specific hydrogel was fabricated by hyaluronic acid and CD conjugates [28]. In vivo analysis demonstrated delivery of the HA/CD hydrogel to HA-receptors in the liver with CDs as bioimaging agents. The drug delivery capability of CD/chitosan hydrogels was further demonstrated for the anticancer drug, 5-Fluorouracil [235]. The drug loaded CD/chitosan hydrogels decreased the cell viability of A549 and NIH 3 T3 cells as estimated by MTT assay after 48 h. Bioimaging of A549 cells were conducted after addition of CD/hydrogel loaded with and without 5-Fluorouracil. The green fluorescence, as illuminated from the hydrogels, could with respect to the nucleus aid in differentiating between living and apoptotic cells.
