The drug delivery of anticancer drug doxorubicin (Dox) was adapted in CD/DNA hydrogel with release of the drug in acidic environment [35]. The cell viability of HeLa cells were further shown to decrease by 65% in contact with the Dox-loaded CD/DNA hydrogel. Another pH-sensitive system with fluorescence sensing was made of PEG/chitosan@CD hybrid hydrogel [236]. The hydrogel further exhibited near infrared light dual responsive delivery of an hydrophobic anticancer drug. A CD/polymer arrangement exhibited multistimuli-responsible properties towards temperature, pH, inorganic ions and organic molecules [237]. The arrangement of hyperbranched polyethylenimine (PEI) functionalized CDs were derived in one-pot microwave reaction, which was further capped with isobutyric amide. With help of the CDs, the molecules had the potential to be loaded into the system, which served for the possibility of drug delivery with bioimaging properties.

Another highly important field within biomedicine is bone tissue engineering that serves to reconstruct defects in the bone. The major components of bone tissues consist of hydroxyapatite and collagen. To regenerate bone tissue, a scaffold is necessary for structural integrity, which can provide adhesion of cells, proliferation and differentiation. After insertion, the scaffold should resorb into harmless components after a suitable period of time required for healing has passed. There are several polymers that can serve for this purpose but they generally lack bioactivity, which aid to produce new bone tissue. CDs were shown to assist osteoblastic cells to grow on a waterborne biodegradable hyperbranched polyurethane scaffold [238]. The in-situ addition of CDs during polymerization of the thermoset further provided enhanced mechanical performance in terms of strength, elongation at break and toughness, which also could be enhanced by increasing the CDs concentration from 0.5 to 1.0 and 1.5 wt%.

Carbon nanofillers have also shown to be bioactive and facilitate the formation of hydroxyapatite e.g. hydroxyapatite crystals on polymer materials. CDs are promising for this purpose due to their nanosize and rich oxygen functionalities, which is believed to attract calcium ions and further lead to the growth of hydroxyapatite on the surface. It was shown that the addition of CDs to PCL matrix induced bioactivity for biomineralization [11]. To improve the mechanical performance of the PCL matrix, CDs were reduced in an efficient microwave process with caffeic acid as reducing agent [151]. At the same time, the ability to induce biomineralization of the PCL surface was maintained. The study further showed that the cell viability of MG63 osteoblast-like cells on the reduced CD/PCL scaffold was comparable to that of neat PCL scaffold [151,239]. Similarly, biopolymers such as starch were utilized as scaffold materials for biomineralization with the aid from covalently attached CDs [34]. CDs were also shown to improve the electrospinnability of starch fibers [12], where the electrospun starch/CD fibers underwent simultaneous degradation. Their combination mimicked the structure and function of extracellular matrices as an artificial interwoven fiber network. PLA and PCL electrospun fibers were similarly fabricated with the addition of CDs for inducing osteo-bioacitivity [240]. Already at low loadings of CD (0.5–2.5%), the strength and stiffness of the fibers were improved compared to the corresponding neat fibers.

CDs show great promise as the building blocks of the next generation of nanocomposite materials due to multifunctionality in combination with low cost, low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Following the green chemistry concept, fabricating CDs from biomass feedstock and through low-energy consuming and environmental-friendly synthesis processes can further trigger a new upsurge of research in material science. This review has demonstrated a comprehensive roadmap from the green synthesis of CDs to the main construction strategies for the fabrication of CD/polymer nanocomposites. Diverse type of biomass sources classified as plant waste and animal waste have been utilized as the CDs precursors with the advantages of being abundant, cost effective and renewable in nature. The so far developed green synthesis techniques mainly include hydrothermal carbonization, microwave irradiation and ultrasound treatment. Currently, physical blending, chemical grafting and in-situ growth are considered as the principle strategies to construct of CD/polymer nanocomposites, among which the in-situ growth strategy stands out as combining the merits of the other two strategies including facile preparation procedure, superior compatibility of each component and strong bonding between CDs and polymer matrix. Furthermore, CD/polymer nanocomposites with cutting-edge applications in the realm of energy storage, environment, and biomedical have been summarized and highlighted.

Despite the remarkable progress with regard to exploring the green synthesis methodologies for production of CDs as well as the applications of CD/polymer nanocomposites made in the past few years, there are still some debatable issues and challenges worthy of future study. 1) Utilizing biomass as a feedstock for the synthesis of CDs has the advantages of wide availability, low cost and eco-friendliness, however the defects, surface state and size distribution of CDs are not precisely controllable due to the variation of substance composition in each batch of biomass. This could affect the subsequent optical and electrical performances in demanding applications. Herein, developing advanced synthesis technologies to precisely control the chemical structure and size of the CDs is of great necessity. 2) The biomass-derived CDs have displayed PL suitable for a wide range of potential applications, but still the biomass-derived CDs with high QYs have been rarely reported. The origin of fluorescence in the biomass-derived CDs is also still unproven. Hence it is desirable to deeply analyze the structure and investigate the PL mechanisms of biomass-derived CDs by combining experimental work with computational simulations in order to enable the synthesis of CDs with high QYs. 3) At present, physical embedding of CDs into the polymer matrix is the mainstream strategy to construct CD/polymer nanocomposites. Although this route demonstrates certain superiority especially in the simplicity of the preparation step, there is the risk of inhomogeneous dispersion and migration of CDs during the use of the product. The burgeoning methods such as in-situ processes can overcome the above-mentioned disadvantages, however they are still on the early-development stage. Methods are also lacking to adequately characterize the CDs when they grow in-situ on the polymer molecules. There is thus great need to develop advanced strategies for the nanocomposite preparation and CD characterization. 4) Although some of the CD synthesis processes are in-line with the requirements of green chemistry, there is still need for greener resources and fabrication processes for production of CD/polymer nanocomposites. The currently utilized polymers are still commonly fossil-based and toxic solvents or high temperature and/or pressure are often involved. 5) A recent study has revealed that under illumination, CDs can degrade into different benzene-related structures which show certain toxicity and could produce hydroxyl and alkyl radicals which further accelerate the degradation process [241]. On the other hand, several studies show that CDs can improve the growth and production of plants, which could be important for future agriculture [242]. These phenomenon and environmental safety should be further investigated especially when considering utilizing the CD/polymer composite materials in environmental and agricultural applications. 6) It has also been recently shown that CDs are highly attractive compounds for photocatalytic hydrogen production. As an example, low cost lignocellulose derived carbon dots could utilize visible light to produce hydrogen from untreated polluted sea and river water [243]. This is highly promising approach for future hydrogen economy and should be further investigated. 7) In similar manner CDs can catalyze solar-driven hydrogen peroxide production. As an example, polymer-supported carbon dots functioned as heterogeneous catalysts for solar-driven production of hydrogen peroxide from water, ethanol and oxygen [244]. A recent study showed that modification of cellulose acetate with CDs provided an effective photocatalytic trigger for sunlight-initiated self-destruction of polymer films [245]. The rapid degradation was connected to the production of hydrogen peroxide, when the films were subjected to UV-light. CD modification of polymers is thus an interesting avenue to explore in the battle against plastic waste. 8) To be utilized as parts such as electrode or SPE in the energy-storage device, in most cases a good electronic transport property is demanded for the CDs/polymer composite materials, which raises the requirement of efficient electronic transfer ability for the CDs. The efficient electronic transfer ability relies in the high crystallinity arrangement of CD structure with few intrinsic defects. Synthesis of CDs free from any intrinsic defects is a huge challenge, especially when utilizing biomass with certain property diversity as CD precursor. The compatibility of the CDs with the polymer matrix is also an important factor in promoting the composite's electronic transfer property, which is yet to be properly investigated. We hope this review will give insights and inspire to free-thinking and new in-depth explorations to further develop the field.
